mission.title: Install and Configure Linode Kubernetes Engine (LKE)
mission.description: This mission guides you through the installation of Linode Kubernetes Engine (LKE), a managed Kubernetes service by Linode. It is ideal for users looking to deploy Kubernetes clusters without managing the underlying infrastructure. Key trade-offs include reliance on Linode's infrastructure and potential costs associated with resource usage.

steps.title: Install the Linode CLI
steps.description:
This step installs the Linode CLI, which is necessary for managing Linode resources from the command line.

```bash

curl -sL https://github.com/linode/linode-cli/releases/download/v2.0.0/linode-cli-2.0.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz | tar xz -C /usr/local/bin

chmod +x /usr/local/bin/linode-cli

```

> This command downloads and extracts the Linode CLI binary.
> Make the Linode CLI executable.

steps.title: Create a Kubernetes Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new Kubernetes cluster on Linode with the specified version and node count.

```bash

linode-cli k8s cluster-create --label my-cluster --region us-east --k8s-version 1.30 --node-count 3

```

> Replace 'my-cluster' with your desired cluster name.

steps.title: Configure Kubeconfig
steps.description:
This step sets up your kubeconfig file to access the newly created cluster.

```bash

linode-cli k8s kubeconfig-view my-cluster --format yaml > ~/.kube/config

```

> This command saves the kubeconfig for your cluster to the default kubeconfig location.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your Kubernetes cluster to ensure they are ready.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see all nodes in the 'Ready' state.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
This step involves setting up autoscaling and monitoring for your cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/linode/examples/cluster-autoscaler.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/monitoring/main/helm-charts/kube-prometheus-stack/templates/monitoring.yaml

```

> This command deploys the Cluster Autoscaler for Linode.
> This command sets up monitoring for your cluster.

uninstall.title: Delete the Kubernetes Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command removes the Kubernetes cluster and all associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss.

```bash

linode-cli k8s cluster-delete my-cluster

```

> Replace 'my-cluster' with your actual cluster name.

uninstall.title: Remove the Linode CLI
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Linode CLI from your system.

```bash

rm /usr/local/bin/linode-cli

```

> This command deletes the Linode CLI binary.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Kubernetes Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your cluster data. This step will upgrade your cluster to the latest version.

```bash

linode-cli k8s cluster-update my-cluster --k8s-version 1.30

kubectl get nodes

```

> Replace 'my-cluster' with your actual cluster name.
> Verify that all nodes are in the 'Ready' state after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates an issue with the kubeconfig file or cluster certificate.

**Fix:**
Regenerate the kubeconfig using 'linode-cli k8s kubeconfig-view my-cluster --format yaml > ~/.kube/config'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No resources found in kube-system namespace'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the necessary components were not deployed.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the Cluster Autoscaler and monitoring components were applied correctly.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient nodes available to fulfill the request'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that there are not enough nodes in the cluster to schedule the pods.

**Fix:**
Increase the node count using 'linode-cli k8s cluster-update my-cluster --node-count <new-count>'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'kubectl: command not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that kubectl is not installed or not in your PATH.

**Fix:**
Install kubectl using 'curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/kubernetes-release/release/$(curl -s https://storage.googleapis.com/kubernetes-release/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl && chmod +x ./kubectl && sudo mv ./kubectl /usr/local/bin/kubectl'.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new node types and improved autoscaling capabilities.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kubernetes versions below 1.22.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.29, ensure that your workloads are compatible with the new node types.

resolution.summary: You should see output from 'kubectl get nodes' showing all nodes in the 'Ready' state, confirming that your cluster is operational.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the necessary tools and resources before starting the installation.
